title: Implement TDummyFirestoreApi stream overrides with controller fan-out
blocks: Write task — writes must re-emit through the collection and document stream registries this task establishes.

status_instructions.on_followup: If you found controller lifecycle gotchas (e.g. listener-count timing), append them to the write task's `side_context`.

description: Override every stream method on TDummyFirestoreApi<T>: `streamAllWithConverter`, `streamAll`, `streamByQueryWithConverter`, `streamByQuery`, `streamDocByIdWithConverter`, `streamByDocId`. Each stream returns a broadcast controller registered in `_collectionControllers` (for list streams) or `_docControllers[id]` (for doc streams). The first emission waits the latency gate and serves the current store snapshot (seeding if empty). Subscriptions unregister themselves via `onCancel`. Writes re-emit instantly through the registries (the write task will wire this end).
context_for_junior: A real Firestore stream emits the initial snapshot after a round-trip to the server, then pushes updates as the data changes. Our dummy imitates that: when you subscribe to `streamAllWithConverter`, we register a broadcast `StreamController<List<T>>`, wait the latency, emit the current store snapshot, and from then on stay silent until something writes to the store. The re-emission on write is done by the write override task — your job here is to hand it a working registry so it can just walk it. Doc streams are per-id — we maintain a `Map<String, Set<StreamController<T?>>>` so when a given id is deleted, every subscriber watching that id gets a `null` emission.
why_it_matters: Streams are how the real `TCollectionService` and `TDocumentService` consume data. If the dummy streams do not fan out correctly on write, every reactive screen will stop updating in dummy mode — defeating the purpose of the feature.
instructions: Override every stream method listed under `dummy_api_class.overrides.streams`. Preserve parameter names and return types from the parent class exactly.

For collection streams: create a `StreamController<List<T>>.broadcast()`, register it in `_collectionControllers`, set `onCancel` to remove it from the registry, and schedule the first emission via `Future.microtask` so it waits the latency gate before adding the initial snapshot. If `_store.isEmpty`, call `_seedStoreIfNeeded()` before emitting.

The first emission rolls the failure gate just like reads. On failure, the controller emits an error via `controller.addError(exception)` rather than returning a TurboResponse — this matches real stream behaviour.
For `streamByQueryWithConverter` and `streamByQuery`, pass the whereDescription through `_applyQueryFilterAndSort` so the query task's later seam is honoured automatically.

For document streams (`streamDocByIdWithConverter`, `streamByDocId`): register a `StreamController<T?>.broadcast()` in `_docControllers[id]` (create the Set if needed), on first emission wait latency + roll failure, then materialise `_store[id]` or generate it if absent, emit once, and stay silent. onCancel removes the controller from the set and deletes the set if it becomes empty.
Ensure `_emitCollections()` (already in skeleton) materialises each stored json via `fromJson` per the spec's `re_emit_rule`.
Ensure `_emitDoc(id, entity)` handles `entity == null` by emitting `null` to every doc-stream listener on that id (for delete support in the next task).
Run `make analyze package=turbo_firestore_api` and the new unit tests.
goals: All stream overrides return broadcast streams with initial-emission latency.
Collection and document controller registries correctly register and unregister on subscribe/cancel.
Stream emission path reuses `_applyQueryFilterAndSort` for filtered streams.
Subsequent re-emissions (from writes in the next task) are instant and bypass the latency gate.
non_goals: No write-side wiring — writes are a later task.
No query filter/sort bodies — the seam stays passthrough.
No backpressure handling beyond broadcast semantics.
No `StreamQueue` or equivalent — raw controllers only.
acceptance_criteria: Unit test: subscribing to streamAllWithConverter returns a broadcast stream that emits exactly one snapshot after at least `dummyDelayDuration`.
Unit test: two concurrent subscribers to streamAllWithConverter each receive the same initial snapshot.
Unit test: cancelling a subscription removes its controller from `_collectionControllers`.
Unit test: streamDocByIdWithConverter emits a materialised entity on first subscribe and stays silent afterwards.
Unit test: with `randomFailurePercentage = 100`, the stream emits an error (via addError) on first emission.
Unit test: after a manual call to `_emitCollections()`, every registered collection controller receives the new snapshot.
Unit test: after a manual call to `_emitDoc(id, null)`, every registered doc controller on that id receives `null`.
constraints: `onCancel` must always clean up; leaked controllers break tests.
No `StreamController.sync: true` — default async semantics match Firestore behaviour best.
Latency is applied exactly once per subscription on the initial emission.
tools: RepoPrompt MCP for reading the real TFirestoreApi stream method signatures

testing.rules: No mocks. Use `expectLater` and `StreamMatcher` from `package:test` for async stream assertions. Set `dummyDelayDuration = Duration.zero` for speed unless a test specifically validates the latency.
scenarios: Given streamAllWithConverter is subscribed, When the subscription is awaited, Then exactly one snapshot is emitted containing `defaultCollectionSize` entities.
Given two subscribers on streamAllWithConverter, When both are awaited, Then both receive the same initial snapshot.
Given a subscribed stream, When the subscription is cancelled, Then `_collectionControllers.length` drops by one.
Given streamDocByIdWithConverter subscribed to id 'x', When the subscription is awaited, Then exactly one non-null materialised entity is emitted.
Given a subscribed doc stream for id 'x', When `_emitDoc('x', null)` is called manually, Then the subscriber receives `null` as the next event.
Given `randomFailurePercentage = 100`, When streamAllWithConverter is subscribed, Then the stream emits an error via addError and then stays silent.
Given one collection subscriber, When `_emitCollections()` is called manually, Then the subscriber receives a snapshot reflecting the current store.
testing.refactor_for_testability: Expose `_collectionControllers.length` via a `@visibleForTesting` getter if direct access is awkward. Do not make state public.

quality_bar.maintainability: Stream setup boilerplate (controller, latency-delayed initial emission, onCancel) is hoisted into one private helper used by every stream override.
quality_bar.dry: Fan-out logic lives in `_emitCollections` / `_emitDoc` and every stream override reuses it.
quality_bar.single_responsibility: Stream overrides manage their controller; they do not generate data directly — they call the seeding helper.
quality_bar.performance: Broadcast semantics are O(listeners) per emission, which is what Firestore delivers anyway.
quality_bar.abuse_resistance: onCancel guarantees controllers do not leak even if the subscriber forgets to close; dispose mops up anything still live.

side_context: Task 07 read overrides: _seedStoreIfNeeded() only runs when _store.isEmpty — if getById* was called first, subsequent list/stream calls see only the lazily-generated single docs, NOT a full collection seed. Streams should reuse this same seeding rule.
_applyQueryFilterAndSort(whereDescription:, input:) seam exists and currently returns input unchanged. Stream overrides should route through it too for query-filtered streams.
_emitCollections() and _emitDoc() are NOT called by read overrides — they are reserved for stream/write tasks.
_getOrCreateStoredDoc(id) generates and stores a doc lazily but does NOT apply timestamps. Seeded docs (via _seedStoreIfNeeded) DO have createdAt/updatedAt timestamps spread across trailing days.
Test runner: ~/Flutter/bin/flutter test works. 62 tests passing. Reads test file at test/apis/t_dummy_firestore_api_reads_test.dart uses _SimpleDto with nullable Timestamp fields.
worklog: Implemented 6 @override stream methods on TDummyFirestoreApi<T>: streamAllWithConverter, streamByQueryWithConverter, streamByQuery, streamDocByIdWithConverter (full in-memory overrides), plus streamAll and streamByDocId (throw UnimplementedError with converter guidance — QuerySnapshot/DocumentSnapshot cannot be constructed without real Firestore).

Added 4 new state fields: _collectionControllerWhereDescriptions (Map<StreamController<List<T>>, String?>) for per-controller query tracking, _rawCollectionControllers (Set<StreamController<List<Map<String, dynamic>>>>) for raw query streams, _rawCollectionControllerWhereDescriptions for their whereDescription metadata.

Each stream override follows the pattern: create broadcast StreamController → register in onListen → schedule initial emission via Future.microtask (latency gate → failure roll → seed if empty → emit snapshot) → unregister in onCancel. Boilerplate is consistent across all overrides per the quality_bar directive.

Refactored _emitCollections() to fan out correctly to both typed and raw collection controllers, applying per-controller _applyQueryFilterAndSort(whereDescription:) so query-filtered streams get correct re-emissions from future write tasks.
Tightened _emitDoc() to clean up empty per-id sets after removing closed controllers.
Extended dispose() to close and clear raw collection controller registries and metadata maps.
Added static _dummyStreamGuidance message used by both UnimplementedError-throwing overrides.

Created test/apis/t_dummy_firestore_api_streams_test.dart with 11 BDD tests covering: broadcast + initial emission, dual-listener parity, controller cleanup on cancel, doc stream initial non-null emission, 100% failure via addError, manual _emitCollections fan-out, manual _emitDoc(null) fan-out, query closure non-invocation, raw query stream emission, streamAll UnimplementedError, streamByDocId UnimplementedError.
dart analyze --fatal-infos: 0 issues. flutter test: 73 tests passed (11 new + 62 existing).
